facts.text: The Aztecs, or Mexica, built one of the most powerful empires in Mesoamerica before the Spanish conquest.
Their capital city, Tenochtitlan, stood on an island in Lake Texcoco where Mexico City now stands.
Tenochtitlan was connected to the mainland by causeways and impressed Spanish observers with its size and engineering.
The Aztec Empire expanded through warfare, tribute networks, and the Triple Alliance of Tenochtitlan, Texcoco, and Tlacopan.
The Mexica worshipped gods such as Huitzilopochtli, Tlaloc, and Quetzalcoatl.
Chinampas, sometimes called floating gardens, were a highly productive agricultural system used around the lakes near the capital.
Aztec society included nobles, priests, merchants, artisans, farmers, and warriors in a complex social hierarchy.
War captives could be used in ritual sacrifice, one of the most discussed and debated aspects of Aztec religion.
Aztec markets were enormous and highly organized, with goods ranging from food and cloth to obsidian tools and cacao beans.
Cacao beans were valuable enough to be used as a form of currency in some contexts.
The Aztecs used pictorial codices to record history, tribute, ritual, and genealogy.
Montezuma II was the Aztec ruler when Hernán Cortés and the Spanish arrived in 1519.
Tenochtitlan fell to the Spanish and their Indigenous allies in 1521, marking the collapse of Aztec imperial power.
Aztec history survives today through archaeology, codices, language, food traditions, and the living cultures of Indigenous Mexico.
The Aztec Empire became one of the most powerful states in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica.
Its political and cultural influence made it one of the best-known civilizations of the region.
Aztec Empire is one of the standout topics people often explore within Aztecs.
Readers interested in Aztecs regularly come across Aztec Empire.
